text: Hello everyone. Today we will show you a minimally invasive abdominoperineal excision of a rectal tumor in a sixty five year old lady which was involving the external sphincter. Neoadjuvant chemoradiation was given prior to surgery.

The surgeon incises the visceral peritoneum over the aorta and continues the incision cranially until reaching the origin of the inferior mesenteric artery. The inferior mesenteric artery is then divided between clips.

The surgeon uses an advanced bipolar device to fuse and divide the sigmoid vein, with clips applied for additional protection. Lateral dissection is performed to mobilize the sigmoid and lower left colon, without mobilizing the upper left colon or performing a high ligation, as part of the abdominoperineal resection.
The left ureter is visible alongside the ovarian vessels. The surgeon performs a complete mesorectal dissection around the rectum, carefully preserving the pelvic nerves.

The surgeon completes the posterior, lateral, and anterior mobilization, leaving the anorectal junction attached to the posterior vaginal wall. The procedure transitions to the perineum, where the anus is separated from the ischorectal tissue, and a cuff of the levator ani muscle is excised. The rectum is delivered through the perineal wound after dividing the anterior attachment to the vagina.
The surgeon resects the specimen and pushes the proximal colonic stump into the abdomen, exteriorizing it to form an end colostomy.
